name: Crafting Materials
description: Collectible resources consumed by recipes, upgrades, repairs, or conversions instead of acting as direct currency.
design_notes: Crafting materials create targeted scarcity. The useful design question is which activities feed each material and which recipes consume it; too many universal materials collapse into coins, while too many narrow materials become inventory clutter.
design_purpose: Give specific activities and biomes their own reward identity.
Gate recipes, upgrades, and repairs through resources that can be forecast and farmed.
Create tradeoffs between spending materials now and saving them for later recipes.
player_fantasy: The player feels resourceful, turning gathered parts into equipment, upgrades, and crafted solutions.

parameters.name: material_id
parameters.description: Canonical identifier used by loot tables, recipes, inventory stacks, and save data.

parameters.name: material_rarity_tier
parameters.description: Tier used for drop rates, UI grouping, and recipe cost expectations.

parameters.name: stack_limit
parameters.description: Maximum amount in one inventory stack before overflow or a new stack is required.

parameters.name: recipe_consumption_count
parameters.description: Quantity removed from inventory when a recipe transaction commits.

parameters.name: duplicate_rule
parameters.description: Handling for material grants that exceed stack or storage limits.

edge_cases: A recipe consumes several material_id values and one stack changes between preview and commit.
A material is removed from a live build but still exists in old saves, loot tables, or recipes.
Inventory is full when a drop grants a required material, so the game needs overflow, conversion, or protected pickup rules.
Shared materials across characters or runs must define whether spending from one profile affects another.
common_bugs: Crafting preview subtracts materials locally and the committed transaction subtracts them again.
Recipe validation checks display names instead of material_id, breaking when localization changes.
Stack split and merge operations lose item_instance_id metadata for enhanced or bound materials.
Obsolete materials remain in loot tables and silently block recipe completion after a content patch.
balancing_notes: Pair each important material with at least one reliable source and one desirable sink so farming feels intentional.
Rare materials should not be required in large quantities for basic progression unless there is pity, conversion, or targeted acquisition.
Recipe costs should be tuned against simulated drop_weight and playtime, not only designer intuition.
accessibility_notes: Show material names, icons, and source hints together so players are not forced to memorize small icons.
Provide recipe pinning or tracking for players who need persistent reminders of required material counts.

implementation_notes.unity: Use material_id in ScriptableObject recipe definitions and inventory stacks; crafting should run as one transaction that validates stack counts, consumes materials, grants output item_instance_id, then saves.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent materials as Resources or JSON rows and keep stack mutation in a single inventory service so UI drag, pickup, and recipe systems share the same validation rules.
implementation_notes.unreal: Store material definitions in DataAssets or DataTables and execute crafting on the authoritative inventory component, rolling back consumption if output grant or persistence fails.
implementation_notes.web: Keep material inventory as normalized maps keyed by material_id; recipe commits should produce a before/after diff that can be persisted, undone, or synced.

example_games.title: Monster Hunter
example_games.note: Uses monster parts and gathered resources for equipment crafting.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses mined and gathered materials as recipe inputs.
